description: Reference outlives the data it points to
rationale: A reference must never outlive its referent; otherwise it becomes a dangling pointer
fix_template: Ensure the referent lives as long as the reference: extend the scope or use owned data

description: Lifetime of returned reference does not match any input lifetime
rationale: Returned references must be tied to an input lifetime to guarantee validity
pattern: fn f(&a x) -> &b T where b != a and b != static
fix_template: Annotate the return type with the same lifetime as the input: `f(x: &T) -> &T`

description: Lifetime annotation can be elided per elision rules
rationale: Redox applies lifetime elision automatically; explicit annotations add noise without benefit
context: single input reference, single output reference

description: Struct contains a reference that may outlive the data it refers to
rationale: Structs holding references must encode the lifetime dependency to prevent dangling references
pattern: S Foo { field: &T } where T lifetime not constrained by Foo
fix_template: Add a lifetime parameter: `S Foo[lt_a] { field: &lt_a T }`
alternatives: Store owned data instead of references

description: Closure captures a reference that does not live long enough
rationale: Closures that outlive their captured references (e.g. returned from function) create dangling pointers
pattern: Closure captures &x; Closure escapes scope of x
fix_template: Use `move` to capture by value, or ensure the reference lives long enough
alternatives: Use owned values in the closure

description: In-place mutation verified: no dangling references during modification
rationale: Confirms that the mutation pattern is safe — reference scopes are properly nested

description: Reference to a temporary value that is dropped immediately
rationale: Temporaries are dropped at the end of the statement; references to them become dangling
fix_template: Bind the temporary to a variable first: `v tmp = expr; v ref = &tmp`

description: Value could be promoted to static lifetime for zero-cost access
rationale: Constant values known at compile time can be placed in static storage, avoiding runtime allocation
alternatives: Keep as runtime value if it may change
